old_claim.note: GPU Parallel Processors provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: GPUs are a major accelerator platform for parallel numerical and deep-learning workloads, but specialized AI accelerators can also be built as TPUs, NPUs, FPGAs, or ASICs.

source_shape.source_locator: Proceedings of the IEEE DNN processing survey, abstract and hardware-platform discussion: DNNs are used in AI applications and require efficient hardware processing; the paper surveys hardware platforms and architectures for DNN processing.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Deep neural-network workloads need efficient hardware processing, and GPUs are one of the hardware platforms used for DNN acceleration.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports an enabling relationship from GPUs to AI accelerators while avoiding a required edge because the survey also frames DNN acceleration as a broader hardware-architecture problem, not a GPU-only lineage.

ontology_before: The graph used a second GPU-like node, gpu_parallel_processors, as the AI accelerator prerequisite even though graphics_processing_units_gpu already represented the same technology family.
ontology_after: The graph rewires AI accelerators to the canonical graphics_processing_units_gpu node and deletes the duplicate GPU node from active data.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: ai_accelerators no longer depends on gpu_parallel_processors; it now depends on graphics_processing_units_gpu.
would_reject_if: A future split distinguishes consumer graphics GPUs from GPGPU accelerator architectures with different source-backed IDs.
The canonical graphics_processing_units_gpu node is rescoped away from programmable/parallel GPU computing.
short_reason: AI accelerators should depend on the canonical GPU node rather than a duplicate GPU-parallel-processors node.
